criteria: The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand the supervised fine-tuning setup, adversarial training formulation, and the RFT-fix baseline.
The agent has read Section 4 completely to understand the suboptimal transfer phenomenon and how RFT delays task adaptation.
The agent has read Section 5 completely to understand Epsilon-Scheduling (Equation 2) and the expected robustness metric (Equation 3).

The agent has read Section 6 and Appendix A completely to extract all experimental settings: backbones, datasets, data splits, augmentations, optimizer, scheduler, APGD configuration, and hyperparameter optimization procedure.
The agent has read Table 1 to check clean accuracy, adversarial accuracy, and expected adversarial accuracy results for all six non-robust backbones across five datasets at epsilon_g = 4/255.
The agent has read Table 2 to check clean accuracy, adversarial accuracy, and expected adversarial accuracy results for all six non-robust backbones across five datasets at epsilon_g = 8/255.
The agent has read Figure 2 to understand the two-hinge linear structure of Epsilon-Scheduling with the three phases defined by T1 and T2.

The agent has read Appendix A to extract training details: AdamW with cosine LR scheduler and warmup, 50 training epochs, APGD with 7 steps for training and 10 for evaluation, images resized to 224x224 with pixel values in [0,1], augmentations (random horizontal flips p=0.5, color jitter with brightness/contrast/saturation=0.25, random rotations).
The agent has read Table 5 to obtain the exact HuggingFace model IDs for all six non-robust backbones (ViT, SWIN, CNX, ClipViT, ClipCNX, R50).
The agent has read Appendix C.1 and Table 7 to understand the ablation study on T1 and T2 hyperparameters of Epsilon-Scheduling.

The plan includes loading all six non-robust pretrained backbone models (ViT-Base, SWIN-Base, ConvNext-Base, ResNet-50, CLIP-ViT, CLIP-ConvNext) from timm and HuggingFace using the exact identifiers in Table 5.

The plan includes loading all five downstream datasets (CUB-200-2011 with 200 classes, Stanford Dogs with 120 classes, Caltech256 with 257 classes, Stanford Cars with 196 classes, FGVC-Aircraft with 100 classes) with train/val/test splits from Hua et al. (2024) for Caltech/Cub/Dogs and Heuillet et al. (2025) for Aircraft/Cars.
The plan includes implementing data preprocessing: resize images to 224x224, pixel values in [0,1], normalization integrated into the model.
The plan includes training augmentations: random horizontal flips (p=0.5), color jitter (brightness, contrast, and saturation set to 0.25), and random rotations.
The plan includes implementing full fine-tuning where both the backbone and the randomly initialized classifier head are trainable parameters.
The plan includes implementing the adversarial training objective (Equation 1) using APGD to generate adversarial perturbations under the ell_inf norm.
The plan includes implementing the RFT-fix baseline, which keeps the perturbation strength fixed at epsilon_g throughout all fine-tuning epochs.
The plan includes implementing the two-hinge linear Epsilon-Scheduling strategy as defined by Equation 2, with parameters T1=12 and T2=37 for a 50-epoch training run.

The plan includes implementing the expected robustness metric (Equation 3) as the area under the accuracy curve from 0 to epsilon_g, estimated using the trapezoidal rule with evaluation steps of 1/255.

The plan includes performing hyperparameter optimization (HPO) for learning rate and weight decay using the ASHA scheduler (Hyperband variant) with a 30-minute budget, searching in the range [1e-5, 1e-1], only for the fix setting, and reusing those hyperparameters for the scheduler setting.

The plan includes conducting the main experiments at two perturbation regimes: epsilon_g = 4/255 (moderate) and epsilon_g = 8/255 (high) for both RFT-fix and RFT-scheduler across all six backbones and five datasets.
The plan includes the ablation study varying T1 and T2 hyperparameters of Epsilon-Scheduling on SWIN-Cars at both perturbation regimes.
The plan includes experiments with robust backbones (RobViT, RobSWIN, RobCNX, RobR50) using RFT-fix and RFT-scheduler on Aircraft, Caltech, and Cars datasets.

The code for loading the six non-robust pretrained backbone models from timm and HuggingFace has been implemented using the exact model IDs specified in Table 5 (e.g., timm/vit_base_patch16_224.augreg_in1k for ViT).
The code for loading and preprocessing the five downstream datasets (CUB-200-2011, Stanford Dogs, Caltech256, Stanford Cars, FGVC-Aircraft) with the correct number of classes has been implemented.
The code applies the correct train/val/test data splits: splits from Hua et al. (2024) for Caltech, Cub, and Stanford Dogs; and from Heuillet et al. (2025) for Aircraft and Stanford Cars.
The code for training data augmentations has been implemented: random horizontal flips with p=0.5, color jitter with brightness, contrast, and saturation all set to 0.25, and random rotations.
The code resizes all images to 224x224 with pixel values in the range [0, 1], and integrates data normalization and standardization directly into the model rather than applying it externally.

The adversarial training objective has been implemented as Equation 1: minimizing the expected max-perturbation cross-entropy loss R_epsilon(f) = E_{(x,y)~D}[max_{||delta||_p < epsilon} L_CE(f(x+delta), y)] using empirical risk minimization.
APGD (Auto PGD) with cross-entropy loss has been implemented for generating adversarial examples during training with 7 steps.
APGD with 10 steps has been implemented for evaluating adversarial accuracy under the ell_inf norm at the target threshold epsilon_g.
The RFT-fix baseline has been implemented: it fixes the perturbation strength at epsilon_g for adversarial example generation across all training epochs.
The two-hinge linear Epsilon-Scheduling has been implemented as Equation 2: alpha(t)=0 for t<T1, alpha(t)=(t-T1)/(T2-T1) for T1<=t<T2, alpha(t)=1 for t>=T2, applied as epsilon(t)=alpha(t)*epsilon_g.
The Epsilon-Scheduling parameters are set to T1=12 and T2=37 for a 50-epoch training run, corresponding to approximately 25% and 75% of total training epochs respectively.

The expected robustness metric has been implemented as Equation 3: Acc_{[0,eps_g]}(f) = (1/eps_g) * AUC_{eps_g}(f), where the AUC is estimated using the trapezoidal rule with evaluation steps of 1/255.

The trapezoidal rule for expected robustness estimation has been implemented as shown in Appendix A: AUC_{4/255}(f) = (1/4) * sum_{i=0}^{3} [Acc_{i/255}(f) + Acc_{(i+1)/255}(f)] / 2, evaluating at each integer 1/255 step from 0 to epsilon_g.
Full fine-tuning is implemented such that both the pretrained backbone parameters and the randomly initialized classifier head parameters are updated during training.
The AdamW optimizer with a cosine learning rate scheduler that includes a warmup period has been implemented for training.

HPO using the ASHA scheduler (a Hyperband variant) has been implemented or integrated, searching learning rate and weight decay in the range [1e-5, 1e-1] with a 30-minute budget, applied only to the fix setting.
Hyperparameters (learning rate and weight decay) found via HPO on the fix setting are reused for the scheduler setting to ensure a fair comparison.
Models are trained for exactly 50 epochs in all experiments.
Results are reported from the model checkpoint at the end of training (epoch 50), not from early stopping or the best validation checkpoint.
The code for loading the four robust backbone models (RobViT, RobSWIN, RobCNX, RobR50) from the ARES repository (github.com/thu-ml/ares/) has been implemented.
The delay time measurement has been implemented: the epoch from which the validation accuracy first exceeds 5% is used as the task adaptation delay time.
The uniform random baseline for direct minimization of expected robustness has been implemented: at each epoch, epsilon is drawn uniformly from U[0, epsilon_g].

The automated epsilon scheduler (auto) has been implemented: it starts with epsilon=0, then initiates a linear increase from T1 (automatically detected as the epoch when validation accuracy converges, with patience of 5 epochs and a tolerance of 2%) to the end of training.

Evaluation with AutoAttack has been implemented as an ensemble of four attacks (untargeted APGD-CE, targeted APGD-DLR, targeted FAB, and black-box Square Attack) for the subset of experiments in Table 6.
The suboptimal transfer characterization experiments (Figure 1) have been executed successfully: RFT-fix with epsilon_g in [1/255, 9/255] for SWIN and ViT across all five datasets.

The training dynamics experiments (Figure 3) have been executed successfully: validation clean accuracy under standard fine-tuning and RFT-fix with epsilon_g in [1/255, 9/255] on multiple datasets to measure task adaptation delay.
The main experiments for Table 1 have been executed successfully: RFT-fix and RFT-scheduler for all six non-robust backbones across all five datasets at epsilon_g = 4/255.
The main experiments for Table 2 have been executed successfully: RFT-fix and RFT-scheduler for all six non-robust backbones across all five datasets at epsilon_g = 8/255.

The robust backbone experiments (Table 3) have been executed successfully: RFT-fix and RFT-scheduler for RobViT, RobSWIN, RobCNX, RobR50 on Aircraft, Caltech, and Cars at both epsilon_g = 4/255 and 8/255.
The ablation study on T1 and T2 (Table 7) has been executed successfully for SWIN-Cars at epsilon_g = 4/255 and 8/255 across all specified (T1, T2) configurations.
The automated scheduler experiment (Table 4) has been executed successfully for SWIN and ConvNext on Aircraft, Caltech, and Cars at both perturbation regimes.
The uniform random baseline experiments (Table 8) have been executed successfully for SWIN, R50, and ClipCNX on Aircraft, Caltech, and Cars at both perturbation regimes.
The AutoAttack evaluation (Table 6) has been executed successfully for SWIN on Cars and Aircraft at epsilon_g = 4/255 and 8/255 for both fix and scheduler settings.

The execution outcomes match Figure 1: suboptimal transfer is highly model- and dataset-dependent, with Aircraft and Cars showing severe degradation (clean accuracy below 5% at moderate epsilon_g) while Caltech remains more robust.

The execution outcomes match Figure 3: standard fine-tuning (epsilon_g=0) shows task adaptation from the first epoch, while RFT-fix with increasing epsilon_g causes progressively longer delays in task adaptation (delay time measured as epoch when validation accuracy exceeds 5%).
The execution outcomes match Figure 9: delay times strongly correlate negatively with final clean accuracy (correlation above 90% across all five datasets for both ViT and SWIN).

The execution outcomes match Table 1: at epsilon_g = 4/255, RFT-scheduler achieves substantially higher clean accuracy and expected adversarial accuracy than RFT-fix across all six backbone-dataset configurations, while sometimes trading off adversarial accuracy.

The execution outcomes match Table 2: at epsilon_g = 8/255, RFT-fix mostly fails (very low clean accuracy) while RFT-scheduler consistently preserves performance, improving clean accuracy, adversarial accuracy (28/30 cases), and expected robustness (all 30 cases).

The execution outcomes match Table 3: for robust backbones, RFT-scheduler consistently improves clean accuracy over RFT-fix but at the cost of reduced adversarial accuracy; expected robustness is still improved for easier tasks (e.g., Caltech).

The execution outcomes match Table 7: when T1=T2 (instant switch from 0 to epsilon_g), performance collapses; increasing T2 yields the most significant improvement in expected robustness; the reported RFT-scheduler results correspond to T1=12, T2=37 marked with (*) in the table.

The execution outcomes match Table 8: the uniform random baseline often results in suboptimal transfer (except on easier datasets like Caltech), confirming that Epsilon-Scheduling is superior to direct uniform minimization of expected robustness.

The execution outcomes match Table 4: the automated scheduler achieves comparable performance to RFT-scheduler on Aircraft, Caltech, and Cars, effectively mitigating suboptimal transfer without requiring manual selection of T1.

The execution outcomes match Table 6: AutoAttack results for SWIN on Cars and Aircraft closely mirror APGD results, confirming that APGD evaluation is a reliable proxy for the stronger ensemble attack.

The execution outcomes match Figure 5: in the aggregated analysis, Epsilon-Scheduling consistently improves expected robustness through significant clean accuracy gains across all backbones and both perturbation regimes.

The execution outcomes match Figure 6: training dynamics show RFT-scheduler achieving high clean accuracy during the first phase (T<T1) and then maintaining stable clean accuracy while building robust accuracy as epsilon increases.

The execution outcomes match Figure 7: the clean loss evolution shows RFT-scheduler initially following standard fine-tuning (low clean loss), while RFT-fix struggles to reduce adversarial loss and keeps clean loss high in early epochs.

The execution outcomes match Table 9: paired t-tests between RFT-fix and RFT-scheduler show statistically significant differences (p < 0.05) for clean accuracy, adversarial accuracy, and expected adversarial accuracy at both epsilon_g = 4/255 and 8/255.
The execution outcomes match Table 12: on ImageNette at both epsilon_g = 4/255 and 8/255, SWIN and CNX with RFT-scheduler achieve higher clean accuracy and expected robustness than with RFT-fix.
